rationale_for_baseline_tier: Acute cholecystitis with systemic inflammation (fever 38.4, tachycardia HR 105) and intractable vomiting with inability to hydrate, on a background of known gallstones — a febrile surgical abdomen requiring emergency-department care for IV fluids, IV antibiotics, bloods, ultrasound and surgical review. The patient is haemodynamically stable (BP 135/85, SpO2 98%, no jaundice, no confusion, no shock), so an emergency ambulance (T5) is not the baseline; T4 (urgent ED, with the adult child available to transport) is correct. Patient-reportable time-critical features — fever plus uncontrolled vomiting preventing all intake — justify exceeding the T3 telehealth ceiling for an unexcludable surgical abdomen. The 000/T5 edges are reserved for genuine deterioration (peritonism/perforation, haematemesis/melaena, cholangitis with rigors/confusion, or shock).

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Pain spreads across the whole abdomen or the belly becomes hard and rigid (generalised peritonism)
escalation_edges.rationale: Generalised peritonism suggests gallbladder perforation — a surgical emergency requiring ambulance transfer.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If the pain suddenly spreads across your whole stomach or your belly goes hard and rigid, call 000 for an ambulance straight away — this could mean the gallbladder has burst.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Vomiting blood or coffee-ground material, or passing black tarry stools
escalation_edges.rationale: Gastrointestinal haemorrhage is an immediate life-threat requiring emergency resuscitation.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you vomit blood or something that looks like coffee grounds, or pass black tarry stools, call 000 immediately.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Yellowing of the eyes or skin with shaking chills or new confusion (ascending cholangitis)
escalation_edges.rationale: Jaundice plus rigors and/or confusion indicates ascending cholangitis and biliary sepsis — a time-critical emergency.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If your eyes or skin turn yellow and you get shaking chills or feel confused, call 000 — this could be a serious infection in the bile ducts.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Feeling faint, cold and clammy, or a racing weak pulse (shock / severe dehydration)
escalation_edges.rationale: Signs of shock from sepsis or severe dehydration require emergency ambulance care.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you feel faint, go cold and clammy, or your heart is racing and you feel like you might collapse, call 000.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Unable to reach the emergency department promptly, or symptoms worsening before arrival
escalation_edges.rationale: If self/carer transport is not feasible or the patient deteriorates, an ambulance is the safe route.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you can't get to the hospital quickly or you get worse on the way, call 000 for an ambulance.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: An adult child is present and able to drive the patient to hospital
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Supports T4 (urgent ED via carer transport) rather than defaulting to an ambulance; but if transport becomes unavailable or the patient deteriorates, switch to 000 (T5).

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Intractable vomiting with inability to hydrate
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Raises dehydration risk and reinforces the need for hospital IV fluids; do not attempt oral rehydration to the point of delaying transfer, and keep the patient nil by mouth for possible surgery.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: Go to the emergency department now for assessment — you need a physical examination, blood tests and an ultrasound, and probably IV fluids and antibiotics. Do not eat or drink anything from now on. Your adult child can drive you. If the pain suddenly spreads across your whole belly or it goes hard and rigid, you vomit blood or pass black tarry stools, your eyes or skin turn yellow with shaking chills, you become confused, or you feel faint — call 000 for an ambulance instead.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: Present to the emergency department now (carer transport); call 000 immediately if any red-flag feature develops
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: The emergency department is open 24 hours — this cannot wait for the GP in the morning.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: Bring a note of the pain timeline (onset after a fatty meal, continuous >8 hours, radiating to the right scapula), the fever and vomiting, the known gallstones, and the paracetamol/ibuprofen taken. Arrange GP or surgical follow-up within 7 days after discharge or surgery.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Managing a febrile surgical abdomen at home or via routine/prompt GP review (antacids, analgesia, wait and see).
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: Progression to gallbladder empyema, gangrene or perforation, ascending cholangitis and biliary sepsis, or gallstone pancreatitis — potentially fatal.

serious_under_triage.failure_description: Directing to same-day GP/urgent care without hospital access delays the bloods, ultrasound, IV antibiotics and surgical review this presentation requires.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: Calling a 000 ambulance for a haemodynamically stable patient who could be driven to ED by the carer — no clinical harm; safe over-triage with some ambulance-resource use.

clinical_uncertainty_handling.key_uncertainty: Whether this is acute cholecystitis, ascending cholangitis, gallstone pancreatitis or another surgical/vascular abdomen cannot be resolved via telehealth without examination, bloods and imaging.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.correct_response_to_uncertainty: Treat as an unexcludable febrile surgical abdomen and escalate for urgent in-person assessment; give conservative safety-netting and nil-by-mouth advice.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.wrong_response_to_uncertainty: Committing to a benign remote label ('just another gallstone attack' or 'gastritis') and advising home management.
